title: B2B Audience Targeting: How Primer is Solving the Unique Marketing Discovery Problem
description:
In this episode of the Click & Mortar podcast, hosts Mike Patterson and Dustin Trout sit down with Keith Delaney, CEO and co-founder of Primer, the innovative platform transforming B2B audience targeting.
Keith shares his journey from high school history teacher to Dropbox marketer to founding Primer, a company that reached $1M in revenue within just 9 months by solving a critical B2B marketing challenge: connecting work emails to personal identifiers that ad platforms actually recognize.
imageAltText: Click and Mortar Podcast: The Power of Ratargeting
bodyHTML:
B2B marketing has long operated with a significant disadvantage compared to its B2C counterparts. While consumer brands enjoy robust targeting capabilities across major ad platforms like Facebook and Google, B2B marketers have largely been confined to LinkedIn, search, and outbound email — channels that quickly become saturated and expensive.

Enter Primer, a company disrupting the B2B marketing landscape by solving one of its most persistent challenges: connecting work identities to personal ones to enable powerful cross-channel targeting.

In a recent episode of the Click & Mortar podcast, hosts Mike Patterson and Dustin Trout sat down with Keith Delaney, CEO and co-founder of Primer, to discuss how his company is revolutionizing audience targeting for B2B marketers.

The Journey from Teacher to Tech Founder

Keith’s path to founding Primer wasn’t a straight line. “It’s been a circuitous journey, as many careers are,” he explained. Starting as a high school history teacher after college, Keith eventually found his way into marketing and landed at Dropbox during its explosive growth phase.

After cutting his teeth in Silicon Valley and working at another Y Combinator startup, Keith realized he didn’t really like having a boss and began exploring entrepreneurial options. While working as a consultant, he noticed he was setting up the same systems over and over again for clients — many involving data engineering and audience creation.

“What if we could take this list of work emails and convert it into an audience on Facebook that would work?” Keith recalled thinking. “I was like, ‘God, somebody’s got to turn this into a business.’”

Despite initial hesitation about entering the crowded MarTech space, Keith and his co-founder built a minimum viable product that reached approximately $1 million in revenue within just nine months.

The B2B Marketing Discovery Problem

The core challenge Primer addresses is fundamental to B2B marketing: 

Most digital advertising platforms are built for consumer targeting.

“B2B kind of gets the short end of the stick when it comes to marketing,” Keith explained. “Most of the distribution channels that exist for getting discovered as a product are built for consumer companies.”

While LinkedIn, search, and outbound email are the traditional B2B channels, they quickly become saturated. The problem lies in connecting professional identities to the personal ones that ad platforms actually use for targeting.

“Everybody has a work email that’s not hard to find, but that’s not the data that Google uses,” Keith noted. “You need that Gmail if you’re going to feed it to Google in order to target me as a CEO, not just as a guy who likes to buy Nikes.”

The Primer Solution: Impressive Match Rates

What makes Primer stand out is its ability to dramatically increase match rates between work identities and personal ones that advertising platforms recognize. According to Keith, “If you just were to upload a list of work emails to Facebook, you get a 10% match rate. With Primer, you can get in the neighborhood of 40 to 60%.”

Dustin Trout, one of the partners here at EIC, reported even better results: “I haven’t seen less than about an 85% match rate with a couple clients and lots of audiences.”

This capability allows B2B companies to break free from the LinkedIn-only approach (where “CPMs are through the roof”) and target their audiences across multiple platforms, including Instagram, Facebook, Google, Reddit, and others.

Ethical Data Sourcing

A natural question arises: How does Primer get this data, and is it legal?

Keith addressed this head-on: “There’s a dark underbelly of third-party data and there always has been, but there also are a lot of companies that are very above board.”

He explained that all the data Primer uses comes from opted-in sources — places where people have given permission for their information to be used. Often, this happens through free products and mobile apps where users agree to terms of service that allow for data sharing.

“If you do accept it, you’re kind of opening yourself up to companies like Primer being able to help really cool B2B companies actually get discovered,” Keith said. “I like a highly personalized, well-targeted ad. I like finding things that are kind of cool and relevant to me.”

Who Benefits Most from Primer?

Primer serves mid-market companies — specifically those with between 100 and 10,000 employees. According to Keith, this sweet spot exists because:

  1. Very large companies often have complex procurement processes that make them difficult to sell to.

  2. Very small companies typically lack the marketing sophistication and budget to fully utilize the platform.

“Most super early-stage companies aren’t going to spend $20,000 a month on ads,” Keith noted. “So why would they lean into Primer in its current state?”

However, he sees potential to develop offerings that would help smaller companies “who are just dipping their toes into advertising” with benchmarks and guidance derived from aggregated customer data.

Advanced Targeting Strategies

During our conversation, Keith shared several sophisticated targeting approaches that Primer enables:

Instagram for B2B Awareness

Despite not being traditionally considered a B2B platform, Keith reported strong engagement on Instagram for awareness campaigns. The challenge is that “you don’t get a ton of last-click converts” because people typically don’t sign up for B2B products from their phones.

To address this measurement gap, Primer builds holdout groups into audiences — essentially creating control groups that don’t see the ads. This allows companies to measure the true lift in conversion rates across devices.

Strategic Google Audience Usage

Keith highlighted two powerful strategies for using Primer audiences in Google campaigns:

  1. Exclusion targeting: “I never want to serve a search ad to my existing users, especially a branded search ad… [or] to anybody outside of my ICP.”

  2. Inclusion targeting: “We’ve got these keywords that are super expensive to bid on. Why don’t we only run them towards folks that we know are qualified through the Primer audience?”

He also mentioned impressive results using Primer audiences as signal in Performance Max campaigns on Google and demand-driven campaigns.

The Future of Primer

Looking ahead, Keith outlined several directions for Primer’s growth:

  1. Expanding channel coverage: Adding more platforms where Primer audiences can be effectively deployed.

  2. Increasing data sources: Beyond Salesforce, HubSpot, and CSV imports, adding connections to workplace tools.

  3. Improving measurement: Developing better ways to assess audience impact and conversion lift.

  4. Mining for insights: Analyzing the performance data across customers to surface actionable recommendations.

Advice for B2B Marketers

Keith closed the conversation with some valuable perspective for all B2B marketers:

“It’s a challenging time for marketers in general, particularly in B2B. It’s this feeling that there’s limited channels,” he noted. “It’s funny how channels kind of act cyclically… everybody’s sort of saying, ‘I feel like right now I hear a lot about events.’ And I’m like, ‘Cool, that’s great.’ And that will be great until everybody starts getting invited to too many events, and then they start saying no to them.”

His recommendation: “That’s the beauty and the challenge of being a marketer — you have to keep your finger on the pulse of what’s changing and evolving and look for the opportunities where other people aren’t paying attention… It’s just that first principles thinking that I think more B2B marketers need to embrace as opposed to a little bit of the herd mentality.”

Want to hear more insights from Keith Delaney and learn how Primer is transforming B2B audience targeting? Check out the full episode of the Click & Mortar podcast here or visit our blog at blog.example.com for more marketing insights and strategies.

title: The Power of Retargeting: How to Turn Website Visitors into Customers
description: Ever wondered why that pair of shoes keeps following you around the internet? That's retargeting - but most businesses are doing it completely wrong. In this episode of Click and Mortar, Mike Patterson and Dustin Trout break down exactly how to build a retargeting strategy that drives real ROI instead of just annoying your potential customers.
imageAltText: Click and Mortar Podcast: The Power of Ratargeting
bodyHTML:
In our latest episode of the Click and Mortar podcast, we do a deep dive into one of marketing's most powerful yet underutilized tools: retargeting. Even Uber drivers are fascinated by this concept once they understand what it means for modern business. Let's explore why retargeting could be the game-changer your marketing strategy needs.

What Is Retargeting, Really?

At its core, retargeting is the art of re-engaging people who have already shown interest in your brand. Think of it as picking up a conversation where you left off. Facebook and Instagram revolutionized this approach by allowing advertisers to reconnect with users across the vast landscape of the internet, creating a more cohesive customer journey.

The Psychology Behind Effective Retargeting

Here's a scenario we've all experienced: You look at a pair of shoes online, and suddenly those exact shoes follow you everywhere you go on the internet. While this approach is common, it's not necessarily effective. As Dustin points out, "If you keep saying 'buy from me' eventually, you're not going to get the result you want."

The real power of retargeting lies in understanding the conversation you're having with potential customers. Instead of showing the same shoes repeatedly, smart retailers might show:

  • Similar styles that match your browsing history

  • Videos of people wearing the shoes in different contexts

  • Customer testimonials

  • Different selling points based on your engagement

The Science of Audience Building

Retargeting isn't just about following people around the internet—it's about building strategic audiences based on behavior. We recommend starting with these fundamental audiences:

1. Website visitors from the last 7 days

2. Social media engagement audiences (people who have commented, liked, or interacted with your posts)

3. First-party database audiences (existing customers, email subscribers, etc.)

The Budget Question: Where Should You Start?

One of the most common questions businesses face is how to allocate their marketing budget between prospecting and retargeting. The answer depends on your current digital footprint:

  • High Traffic Businesses (20,000+ monthly visits)

  • Focus primarily on retargeting—it's your low-hanging fruit for immediate ROI

  • 80/20 retargeting budget to prospecting budget

  • Medium Traffic Businesses (≈1,000 monthly visits)

  • Start with a 50/50 split between prospecting and retargeting.

  • Low Traffic Businesses (200 monthly visits)

  • Invest more heavily in prospecting to build your retargeting audiences

  • 80/20 prospecting budget to retargeting budget

Advanced Targeting: The Conversation Approach
Think of retargeting as a sophisticated conversation. Someone who spends four minutes on your product page needs a different message than someone who bounces after eight seconds. This is where the art of retargeting truly shines.

Consider these engagement levels:

  • Shallow engagement: Focus on building awareness and addressing initial objections

  • Medium engagement: Provide more detailed information and social proof

  • Deep engagement: Move toward conversion with specific offers and calls to action


The Frequency Sweet Spot

A crucial aspect often overlooked is frequency—how often your ads appear to the same person. We recommend:

  • 1-2 exposures per day maximum

  • Under 10 exposures in a seven-day period

  • Adjusting budget allocation if frequency exceeds these guideline


Leveraging First-Party Data

Don't forget about the gold mine of data you already have. Your existing customer database can be used to create powerful retargeting campaigns:

  • Target customers who haven't purchased in 6+ months

  • Create specific messages for different stages of your sales pipeline

  • Use customer data to inform your messaging and offers


The Bottom Line

Retargeting represents the bottom three-quarters of your marketing funnel, where the real conversion magic happens. While prospecting casts a wide net, retargeting is where you nurture relationships and drive actual sales.

As Dustin puts it, "Look at the data and keep asking yourself, how would I converse with this person if they were a real-life human being?" This human-centric approach to retargeting might be exactly what your digital marketing strategy needs.

title: The Four Keys to Effective Lead Generation and Where to Spend Your Time
description: In this episode of the Click and Mortar podcast, Mike Patterson and Dustin Trout break down the four critical components every successful lead generation program needs. Whether you're running campaigns in-house or working with an agency, these insights will help you optimize your lead generation efforts.
imageAltText: Click and Mortar Podcast: 4 Keys to Effective Lead Generation
bodyHTML:
In today’s digital landscape, generating quality leads consistently isn’t just about running ads or sending emails. It requires a systematic approach that nurtures potential customers from their first interaction to final conversion. 

In our recent Click and Mortar podcast, we break down down the four essential components of successful lead generation. We hope our insights provide a roadmap for businesses looking to optimize their lead generation efforts.

These are the four areas, where we recommend you focus:

Image coutesy of Napkin.ai

Strategic Advertising: The Gateway to Lead Generation
The journey begins with advertising — your first touchpoint with potential customers. But it’s not just about putting ads out there; it’s about strategic placement and optimization. The key areas to focus on include:

  • Channel effectiveness across platforms 
    (Google Search, Display, Meta, TikTok, TV ads)

  • Audience targeting optimization (prospecting vs. retargeting)

  • Creative performance analysis (video vs. static, vertical vs. square formats)

  • Cost per lead monitoring as the North Star metric

Weekly analysis of these elements ensures your advertising spend generates quality leads efficiently. As Dustin emphasizes, “What channels are driving efficient leads? From within there, what are the audiences that are working?”

Nurturing Sequences: The Art of Following Up
Here’s a shocking statistic shared in the podcast: only 3–7% of initially interested customers buy immediately. This makes your nurturing sequence crucial for converting the remaining 93–97%. Your nurturing strategy should consider:

  • Communication medium (email vs. text)

  • Timing optimization (frequency and spacing of messages)

  • Content variety (questions vs. brand storytelling)

  • Pipeline progression tracking

The goal is to maintain engagement while moving leads through your sales pipeline. Testing different approaches helps identify what resonates best with your audience.

Ongoing Communication: Building Long-term Relationships
Beyond initial nurturing, regular communication keeps your brand top-of-mind. 

We recommend spending time on all of these topics in your ongoing communications:

  • Weekly emails and texts (minimum)

  • Diverse content mix

  • Customer testimonials

  • Employee spotlights

  • Company updates

Contrary to common concern, sending 3–4 communications per week isn’t spam if the content provides value. The key is monitoring unsubscribe rates and maintaining content quality.

ROI and Data Analytics: Measuring What Matters
The final pillar focuses on understanding your results and optimizing performance. 

Key considerations include:

  • Lead-to-sale conversion rates by channel

  • Sales pipeline barrier analysis

  • Customer acquisition cost trending

  • Overall conversion rate improvements

“If you can get customers cheaper, you’re going to be more profitable,” notes Trout. This makes regular analysis of your metrics crucial for ongoing optimization.

The Dashboard Difference
One crucial insight from our discussion: managing these four pillars becomes overwhelming without proper tracking. A comprehensive dashboard that surfaces key metrics and opportunities is essential for success. 

It helps identify:

  • Performance trends

  • Optimization opportunities

  • Conversion bottlenecks

  • ROI by channel and campaign

Making It Work For Your Business
Implementing these four pillars requires dedication and systematic attention. Each week you should focus on the following areas, spending at least 30 minutes in these crucial areas:

1. Weekly review of advertising performance

2. Regular testing of nurturing sequences

3. Advance planning of ongoing communications

4. Monthly analysis of conversion metrics

5. Dashboard implementation for efficient monitoring

The key is maintaining consistency while continuously optimizing each component based on data-driven insights.

Conclusion
Effective lead generation isn’t about mastering just one aspect — it’s about coordinating all four pillars into a cohesive strategy. By focusing on advertising, nurturing sequences, ongoing communication, and data analytics, businesses can build a lead generation machine that consistently delivers results.

Want to dive deeper into these strategies? 

Watch the full episode of the Click and Mortar podcast or visit our blog for more insights into digital marketing and lead generation best practices.

Remember: the difference between good and great lead generation often lies in the details and systematic execution of these four pillars.

title: The Marketing Funnel Blueprint: Create Better Ads That Convert
description: In this episode of Click and Mortar, Mike and Dustin break down exactly what creative content you need at each stage of your marketing funnel. Stop guessing about what content to create and start building ads that convert.
imageAltText: Click and Mortar Podcast: Marketing Funnel Creative
bodyHTML:
For digital marketers, few questions are more common than "What kind of creative should I create?" and "How much content do I actually need?" The answers often feel elusive, leading many businesses to create content without a clear strategy or framework.

But here's the truth: successful marketing isn't about having the most content – it's about having the right content at the right stage of the customer journey.

Breaking Down the Marketing Funnel

Through years of running successful digital campaigns, we've developed what we call the Marketing Funnel Blueprint. This framework breaks down exactly what kind of creative you need at each stage of the customer journey, from first touch to final conversion.

Let's dive into each stage:


The Awareness Stage: Making That First Impression

The awareness stage is all about introducing your brand and making people aware of the problem you solve. This is where you need to grab attention and clearly communicate your unique selling proposition.

Video content shines particularly bright at this stage. Why? Because video allows you to:

- Quickly capture attention in crowded social feeds

- Tell your brand story effectively

- Build engagement metrics for future retargeting

- Create audiences based on watch time and engagement

The key here is clarity. Rather than being vague (like saying "we fix cars"), be specific ("we do oil changes in 30 minutes or less"). This clarity helps potential customers self-identify and engage with your content if it's relevant to their needs.

The Interest Stage: Building Trust Through Proof

Once someone knows who you are, they need a reason to trust you. This is where social proof becomes crucial.

At the interest stage, focus on:

- Customer testimonials and reviews

- Data points about your business

- Proof that your products or services work

- Stories from satisfied customers

Think about it like a sales conversation or even a first date. After introducing yourself, people naturally want to know more about you. What makes you interesting? What do others say about you? This is your chance to answer those questions through your creative.

The Desire Stage: Making Them Want It

At the desire stage, you need to focus on how your product or service will actually change someone's day or life.

This is where you:

- Highlight specific benefits

- Show transformation stories

- Demonstrate real-world applications

- Start introducing concrete solutions

The key question to answer here is "How will this make their day better?" Use social proof and testimonials that specifically speak to the transformation or improvement customers experience.

The Action Stage: Driving the Conversion

Finally, we reach the action stage. This is where many businesses start – but it should actually be your final piece.

At this stage:

- Present clear, compelling offers

- Create urgency (when authentic)

- Make the next steps crystal clear

- Remove any final barriers to purchase

The key is to make taking action feel like an obvious choice. By this point, you've built awareness, established trust, and created desire – the offer should feel like a natural next step.

Starting Small: You Don't Need Everything at Once

One of the biggest misconceptions about marketing funnels is that you need dozens of pieces of content at each stage. In reality, you can start with just one or two pieces of creative at each funnel stage. The key is ensuring each piece hits the right message points for that stage of the journey.

The Power of Saying the Same Thing Differently

Remember: the name of the game is finding different ways to communicate the same core message. Extract what makes your business unique and find multiple ways to express it across your funnel stages. This keeps your message consistent while avoiding ad fatigue.

---

Want to dive deeper into exactly how to implement this framework in your business? We break down specific examples and provide our complete Marketing Funnel Blueprint in the full episode.

Download our free Marketing Funnel Blueprint document to start mapping out your own creative strategy. It's the same framework we use with our clients to structure successful campaigns.

Remember: the most effective marketing isn't about having the most content – it's about having the right content at the right time in the customer journey.

title: A Digital Marketing Transformation: Veteran's Guide to Navigating Change
description:
In this episode of the EIC Agency Podcast, join Jim Piazza as he breaks down his journey from traditional advertising to digital marketing mastery. Learn how to:

✓ Balance data and creativity in marketing
✓ Navigate digital transformation strategies
✓ Build meaningful business metrics
✓ Understand the art of change management
imageAltText: Click and Mortar Podcast: Jim Piazza of Spartaco
bodyHTML:
In the rapidly evolving world of digital marketing, staying ahead isn't just about adopting new technologies—it's about understanding the delicate balance between data and human connection. Jim P's journey through marketing's transformative landscape offers a masterclass in strategic innovation.


The Evolution of a Marketing Career

Jim's professional trajectory reads like a roadmap of marketing's digital revolution. Starting in traditional advertising in the 1990s, he witnessed the emergence of digital platforms firsthand. His career spans critical sectors—retail at Lowe's, distribution at MSC Industrial, and now manufacturing—each transition teaching valuable lessons about adaptability.


The Art and Science of Marketing

One of Jim's most profound insights is the need to balance data-driven metrics with creative communication. "It's not just about the algorithm you can get from an AI tool," he explains. "There's more emotion in humans than there is just in data."


Key Principles of Digital Transformation

1. Understand Your Unique Business Metrics

Generic KPIs are meaningless. Jim emphasizes creating metrics specifically tailored to your organization's unique needs. At MSC Industrial, he developed a strategy focusing on digital customer segments that ultimately grew a $200 million business.

2. Embrace Gradual Change

Jim's mantra? "Slow down to speed up." Digital transformation isn't about radical overnight shifts but strategic, patient implementation. Understanding your team's readiness and managing their transition is crucial.

3. Focus on Meaningful Engagement

Digital marketing success isn't about vanity metrics. It's about reaching the right audience with the right message. Jim's approach involves deeply understanding stakeholders—from sales representatives to customer buyers.


A Transformative Success Story

Perhaps most compelling is Jim's experience at MSC Industrial. By transitioning small customers to a digital platform with targeted loyalty programs, he not only outpaced company growth but also won a rare sales award typically reserved for salespeople.

Lessons for Modern Marketers from that experience

- Never stop learning

- Look beyond surface-level data

- Recognize that marketing is both an analytical and creative discipline

- Build strategies that connect genuinely with your audience


The Future of Digital Marketing

As industries continue evolving, Jim's journey demonstrates that successful marketing requires continuous adaptation. It's about understanding technological tools while maintaining the human touch.

Whether you're a seasoned marketing professional or just starting your journey, there's something to learn from Jim's remarkable career.

title: Why Are Ads the Best Lead Generation Tactic
description: Stop throwing money at digital ads without a strategy! In this episode, we break down the science of determining your ideal ad spend, minimum platform budgets, and the timeline you need for true ROI. Learn why patience matters and how to work backwards from your business goals to set a winning ad budget.
bodyHTML:
In today’s digital-first world, businesses are bombarded with choices about how to market their services and products. The debate often boils down to organic social media efforts versus paid advertising. While organic strategies may seem cost-effective, they frequently fall short in scalability, reach, and impact. In contrast, paid ads present a streamlined, measurable, and highly scalable solution for lead generation.

In the latest episode of the Click and Mortar podcast, digital marketing experts Dustin Trout and Mike Patterson break down why paid advertising is the cornerstone of a successful growth strategy, particularly for small and medium-sized businesses.

Why Paid Ads Outperform Organic Efforts

One of the major challenges with organic social media efforts is the limited reach. Social media platforms typically show posts to only 15% of your followers, most of whom are friends and family rather than prospective customers. This significantly limits the ability to drive meaningful engagement and new business. Moreover, creating effective organic content requires substantial time and effort—planning, drafting, designing, posting, and monitoring—all without guaranteed results.

Paid ads eliminate these constraints. By using paid channels, businesses can instantly expand their reach to targeted audiences beyond their immediate followers. Paid advertising is highly scalable, enabling businesses to allocate budgets based on growth goals, market demand, or seasonal priorities. Unlike organic posting, where results can be erratic, paid ads ensure predictability and control over outcomes.

The Power of Measurable Metrics

One of the strongest arguments for paid advertising is its data-driven nature. With tools available across platforms, businesses can measure essential metrics like:

  • Cost per Lead (CPL): Understand how much you're spending to acquire each lead.

  • Conversion Rates: Determine how effectively leads turn into paying customers.

  • Return on Investment (ROI): Evaluate whether ad spend is contributing to your bottom line.

As Dustin Trout highlights, “Once you know the cost to acquire a customer, you have the framework to set realistic goals and allocate the budget necessary to achieve them.” This level of precision allows businesses to forecast results and make informed decisions, something organic strategies simply cannot match.

Adaptability and Flexibility

Testing and experimentation are crucial in digital marketing, and paid ads make this process seamless. Businesses can explore new platforms, refine their messaging, and target specific audiences without major investments in infrastructure or personnel. Whether it’s testing YouTube ads or reaching new demographics on TikTok, paid advertising offers unparalleled flexibility.

In contrast, organic efforts often require onboarding new staff or reallocating internal resources, which can be time-intensive and emotionally taxing. Paid ads allow businesses to test and iterate rapidly, scaling successful campaigns and pivoting away from underperforming ones.

Why Paid Ads Attract Investors

For businesses seeking funding, having clear acquisition metrics is non-negotiable. Investors prioritize companies that can demonstrate a measurable, scalable growth strategy. Paid ads deliver this by providing concrete data about user acquisition costs and campaign effectiveness. Relying solely on organic posting to drive growth is often seen as unsustainable and unappealing to potential investors.

Getting Started

The good news? You don’t need a massive budget to start leveraging paid ads. Even a modest investment can yield significant insights into your audience and help you refine your approach. As Dustin Trout advises, “Don’t just hire a social content creator to post. Run ads and learn.”

By prioritizing paid advertising, businesses can achieve results faster, more efficiently, and with greater predictability than ever before.

Take the Next Step

If you’re ready to grow your business with a smarter, scalable approach, it’s time to explore paid advertising. Whether you’re a small business owner or a seasoned entrepreneur, paid ads provide the tools you need to expand your reach, track your progress, and achieve your goals.

title: The True Cost of Digital Advertising: Why Your Ad Spend Strategy Matters
description: Stop throwing money at digital ads without a strategy! In this episode, we break down the science of determining your ideal ad spend, minimum platform budgets, and the timeline you need for true ROI. Learn why patience matters and how to work backwards from your business goals to set a winning ad budget.
bodyHTML:
Many businesses approach digital advertising with a simple directive: "Here's the budget, make it work." But as veterans in the digital marketing space, we've learned that this approach often leads to disappointment and wasted resources. The real question isn't how much you should spend—it's what you're trying to achieve.

Understanding Your Investment Timeline

Digital advertising isn't a sprint; it's a marathon. One of the most common mistakes we see is businesses pulling the plug too early, typically after just a couple of months of spending. The reality? You need at least three to six months to truly evaluate the effectiveness of your digital advertising strategy.

This timeline isn't arbitrary. It allows for:

- Algorithm optimization

- Data collection and analysis

- Consumer behavior patterns to emerge

- Campaign refinement and iteration

The Minimum Viable Ad Spend

While budgets vary by industry and goals, there are some baseline numbers you should consider. Our experience shows that effective campaigns typically require $50-100 per day per platform to gain meaningful traction. This isn't just about throwing money at platforms—it's about giving your campaigns enough fuel to generate statistically significant data.

Starting Small: The Education Phase

For businesses new to digital advertising, we recommend a different approach. Start with a modest budget of around $500, focusing not on immediate ROI but on education. Learn to understand:

- Key performance indicators (KPIs)

- Impression metrics

- Click-through rates

- Audience behavior patterns

This educational phase is crucial. It's better to learn the landscape with a smaller budget than to burn through a large one without understanding the metrics that matter.

The Goal-First Approach

Instead of starting with a budget number, start with your business objectives. Want to generate five sales this month? Great. Now let's work backward:

1. What's the value of each customer?

2. How many prospects do you need to meet to close five sales?

3. What's the cost to get those prospects in the door?

4. What's your acceptable customer acquisition cost?

This reverse-engineering approach helps align your spending with actual business outcomes rather than arbitrary budget numbers.

The ROI Timeline

Here's a hard truth: don't expect immediate profitability. Most successful campaigns take 60-90 days to break even. However, once you hit that sweet spot—understanding your metrics, optimizing your campaigns, and seeing consistent results—the scaling potential becomes significant.

Moving Forward

Remember: Rome wasn't built in a day, and neither is a successful digital advertising strategy.

The key is to:

- Set clear, measurable goals

- Start with education if you're new to the space

- Commit to minimum effective spending levels

- Give your campaigns proper time to mature

- Continuously measure and adjust based on data

title: The Hidden Math Behind Profitable Marketing: Why ROI Tracking Isn't Optional Anymore
description: Stop throwing money at marketing without knowing if it works! In this episode of the Click and Mortar Podcast, Mike and Dustin from EIC Agency break down exactly how to track and calculate your marketing ROI. Whether you're just starting out or looking to optimize your current tracking systems, this episode gives you the exact formula for success.
imageAltText: Key Components of Lead Generation ROI
bodyHTML:
In the fast-paced world of digital marketing, there's a stark difference between businesses that grow consistently and those that struggle: the ability to track and measure their marketing ROI. Yet surprisingly, most companies still fly blind when it comes to understanding whether their marketing efforts are actually generating profit.

The Reality Check

"Most businesses don't even have a way to track if sales are happening from ads versus organically," shares Dustin from EIC Agency in their recent Click and Mortar podcast episode.

This observation hits at the heart of a critical problem in modern marketing – the gap between spending and accountability.

Beyond Vanity Metrics

While many marketers focus on impressions, clicks, and engagement rates, these are what Dustin calls "vanity metrics."

The real story of marketing success lies in a more fundamental equation:

1. How much are you spending?

2. How many leads is that spend generating?

3. What's your cost per lead?

4. How many leads convert to customers?

5. What's your average order value (AOV)?

Breaking Down the ROI Formula

Let's make this concrete. Mike from EIC breaks down a simple example:

- You spend $1,000 on advertising

- This generates 20 leads

- Your cost per lead is $50

- If 50% of leads convert, you get 10 customers

- Multiply by your average order value to get total revenue

- Divide revenue by ad spend for your ROI

This straightforward calculation reveals the true profitability of your marketing efforts.

The Technology Stack That Makes It Possible

The journey to ROI tracking doesn't have to be complex. According to the EIC team, you can start with something as simple as a Google Sheet. As your needs grow, you can graduate to a CRM system that helps you:

- Centralize lead data

- Track customer interactions

- Automate nurturing sequences

- Monitor sales conversions

- Calculate revenue attribution

Channel-Specific Insights

One of the most valuable aspects of proper ROI tracking is understanding performance across different marketing channels. As Dustin notes, "Google might be a hundred and twenty dollar cost per lead but Facebook might be a ten dollar cost per lead." However, conversion rates might tell a different story, making the more expensive channel potentially more profitable.

The Hard Truth About Implementation

Setting up proper ROI tracking isn't always fun. It requires dedication, attention to detail, and often a fundamental shift in how you approach marketing. But as Dustin emphasizes, "It isn't fun work, but once you do it, you can grow so much more profitably."

From Expense to Investment

The ultimate goal is to transform marketing from a cost center into a profit center. When you have clear visibility into your ROI:

- You can make data-driven decisions about where to invest

- Marketing becomes predictable and scalable

- You can optimize campaigns based on actual revenue impact

- Budget discussions become about growth rather than costs

The Path Forward

If you're not tracking your marketing ROI yet, start with the basics:

1. Set up a simple system to track leads

2. Monitor your conversion rates

3. Calculate your average order value

4. Document your marketing spend

5. Begin measuring the results

As Mike emphasizes, "Track those key metrics like your life depends on it, because your life does depend on it. Your business depends on it."

Ready to Take Action?

title: The Art and Science of Creating Irresistible Marketing Offers
description: Whether you're a small business owner, marketer, or entrepreneur, this episode is packed with actionable insights to elevate your marketing game. Free offer creation guide included!
imageAltText: Unlock the Secrets of Killer Marketing Offers!
bodyHTML:
In the world of marketing, there's one element that can make or break your entire strategy: the offer. But what exactly makes an offer truly compelling? It's not as simple as slapping a discount on your product and calling it a day.

The Offer Misconception

Most businesses fall into the trap of thinking an offer is just a percentage off. "25% discount!" they shout, wondering why customers aren't breaking down their doors. But here's the truth: discounts are the laziest form of marketing.

Dustin Trout, a marketing expert, puts it bluntly: "Everyone has an offer, they just don't realize it." Think about it – even a gallon of gas at $3.29 is an offer. The real question is: Is it a good offer?

Understanding the Anatomy of a Great Offer

Creating a killer offer is part art, part science. It requires you to:

1. Know Your Audience Intimately

   Understanding what your customers truly want is the foundation of any great offer. It's not about what you think they need, but what they're actively seeking.

2. Create Tangible Value

   Customers need to quickly and easily understand the value they're getting. If they have to work too hard to figure out the benefit, they're gone.

3. Go Beyond Discounts

   Here are some creative offer strategies:

   - Exclusive in-store products

   - Loyalty programs and VIP clubs

   - Product personalization

   - Bundling complementary products

   - Price match guarantees

The Power of Value-Add Offers

Take a cue from John Taffer of Bar Rescue: instead of discounting, find ways to provide something of value for free. For instance, if someone buys a hat, what shirt would complement it? Purchasing a mattress? Include a pillow set.

The Marketing Mathematics of Offers

Here's a mind-blowing stat: a well-crafted offer can transform your marketing efficiency. The difference between a generic offer and a targeted one can mean going from a $12 lead to a $3 lead – essentially 4x-ing your marketing returns.

Leveraging AI in Offer Creation

Modern marketers have a secret weapon: AI. Use artificial intelligence to:

- Brainstorm offer ideas

- Generate multiple variations

- Refine and optimize your approach

The Essential Call to Action

Remember, a great offer isn't complete without a clear call to action. Tell your customers exactly what to do next – whether it's clicking a link, visiting a landing page, or claiming a special deal.

Continuous Optimization is Key

Marketing is never a "set it and forget it" game. Continuously:

- Test different offer variations

- Gather data

- Refine your approach

- A/B test to understand what resonates

Your Free Offer Creation Toolkit

We've created a fantastic starter guide to help you kickstart your offer creation journey. It includes:

- Offer categories

- Specific offer examples

- Strategies to move beyond basic discounts

Ready to Transform Your Marketing?

Offers are more than just a transaction – they're an opportunity to connect, provide value, and stand out in a crowded marketplace.

title: EIC Agency Podcast: Dr. Augustine Fou talks Bots and Other Digital Advertising Pitfalls
description: Dr. Augustine Fou reveals the hidden world of digital ad fraud: 50% bot traffic, fake audiences, and how small businesses can protect their marketing dollars. Learn insider strategies to combat bot fraud, understand true ad performance, and maximize your advertising ROI. Uncover the truth behind programmatic advertising, audience targeting, and conversion metrics.
imageAltText: Dr. Augustin Fou on the EIC Podcast
bodyHTML:
In the ever-evolving landscape of digital marketing, there’s a silent predator wreaking havoc on businesses’ advertising efforts. Dr. Augustine Fou, a leading expert in ad fraud, is blowing the whistle on a problem that’s costing companies billions: bot traffic.

The Shocking Scale of Digital Deception

Imagine spending half of your marketing budget on advertisements that no human ever sees. Sounds impossible? Think again. Dr. Fou’s research reveals that approximately 50% of digital ad traffic is generated by bots, not real people. But here’s where it gets even more alarming: some fraudulent websites have 100% bot traffic, meaning zero human visitors.

How Did We Get Here?

The rise of programmatic advertising created a perfect storm for fraud. Around 2012–2013, advertisers sought an easier way to place ads across thousands of websites. Programmatic exchanges became the solution, allowing companies to buy ad space from multiple sites through a single platform. However, this convenience came with a massive blind spot.

Bad actors quickly exploited this system by creating thousands of fake websites using simple WordPress templates. These sites flood the exchange with fraudulent traffic, siphoning millions from even the largest advertisers.

The Myth of Audience Targeting

Dr. Fou exposes another critical issue: audience targeting. Advertisers believe they’re precisely targeting specific demographics, but bots have learned to game the system. For instance, pharmaceutical companies thinking they’re targeting oncologists might actually be showing ads to sophisticated bots that have learned to mimic desired audience behaviors.

Real-World Impact for Small Businesses

For small and local businesses, the stakes are even higher. Limited budgets mean every marketing dollar counts.

Dr. Fou offers practical advice:

1. Stick to main platforms where humans are actively engaged
2. Turn off audience networks on platforms like Facebook and Google
3. Start small, measure results, and scale cautiously
4. Use analytics to verify the quality of clicks and conversions

The Future of Digital Advertising

The impending death of third-party cookies might actually be a blessing. It could reduce the ability of bots to manipulate audience targeting and over-attribute sales to digital ads.

Practical Solutions: What Can You Do?

Dr. Fou recommends several strategies:

- Implement “turn-off experiments” to measure true advertising impact
- Focus on conversion velocity instead of raw numbers
- Buy directly from known media sellers
- Use analytics tools to verify traffic quality

The Connected TV (CTV) Conundrum

Even emerging channels like Connected TV aren’t immune. While direct buys from legitimate channels like Food Network have minimal fraud, programmatic CTV can be riddled with bot-generated fake requests.

A Call to Action for Marketers

Small business owners can lead the charge in more responsible digital advertising. By being vigilant, using data-driven approaches, and questioning traditional metrics, we can start to clean up the digital advertising ecosystem.
